overall.outcomeDetail: The student did not agree to pay the 4000 rupee seat fee because the counsellor did not provide detailed module information or a clear next‑step invitation.

rubric.label: Rapport & Opening
rubric.justification: Counsellor never greeted or built personal connection, only gave brief prompts.

rubric.label: Needs Discovery
rubric.justification: Asked a generic "What are you looking for?" but did not probe deeper into the student's goals or pain points.

rubric.label: Objection Handling
rubric.justification: When the student requested specific module details, the counsellor gave a vague answer instead of addressing the concern.

rubric.label: Product Knowledge & Accuracy
rubric.justification: Provided no concrete information about algorithms, tools, case studies or leadership frameworks; answers were ambiguous.

rubric.label: Closing & Next Steps
rubric.justification: No attempt to secure commitment, set next steps, or discuss the seat fee.

rubric.label: Communication & Empathy
rubric.justification: Responses were short and lacked empathy; the counsellor did not acknowledge the student's specific interests.

phaseBreakdown.name: Student Introduction
phaseBreakdown.summary: Opening – counsellor prompted the student to introduce themselves but did not greet or create a warm opening.
phaseBreakdown.didWell: Requested the student's introduction, giving a chance to speak.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Start with a friendly greeting, use the student's name, and share a brief personal intro to build rapport.

phaseBreakdown.name: Course Information
phaseBreakdown.summary: Discovery – asked a single broad question about what the student is looking for, without deeper probing.
phaseBreakdown.didWell: Asked "What are you looking for?" which invited the student to state goals.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Follow up with targeted questions about current role challenges, skill gaps, and employer expectations.

phaseBreakdown.name: Concerns and Objections
phaseBreakdown.summary: Presentation – gave a generic description of the program’s foundation‑first approach, no specifics on modules.
phaseBreakdown.didWell: Mentioned that the program starts from foundations and goes deeper.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Provide detailed curriculum points: algorithms covered, tools used, case studies, leadership frameworks, and real‑world projects.

phaseBreakdown.name: Closing
phaseBreakdown.summary: Closing – no attempt to secure commitment, outline next steps, or discuss the 4000 ₹ seat fee.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Summarize benefits, ask for the student's decision, explain enrollment steps and the seat fee, and schedule the next call.

strengths.point: Attempted to ask the student’s goals

improvements.quote: "Yes, this program is designed for all we start from foundations…"
improvements.suggestion: List exact topics, algorithms, tools, case studies, and leadership models covered in each module.

improvements.point: Close the conversation with clear next steps
improvements.suggestion: After answering questions, recap benefits, ask for confirmation, explain the 4000 ₹ seat fee, and schedule enrollment or a follow‑up.
Begin with a warm greeting, use the student's name, and mirror their enthusiasm before diving into details.

transcript.text: I’m a data analyst with around three years of experience at a mid‑size firm. I took the qualifier test because I was curious whether the IIM Ranchi program could offer any fresh insights or help me progress further in my career.
introduce yourself

I’m a data analyst with about three years of experience in analytics at a mid‑size company. I cleared the qualifier test because I wanted to see if an IIM Ranchi certification could add real value to my current skill set and career growth.
What are you looking for?

I’m looking to deepen my expertise in applying machine‑learning and AI at a strategic level – essentially moving from execution to leadership on analytics projects. I’d like to know how the ML/AI modules are structured for someone already working in the field, and how employers typically view an IIM Ranchi certificate for an experienced analyst.
Thank you.

That makes sense. Could you tell me exactly what topics the “Machine Learning for Business — Foundations” and “Advanced Analytics, AI Applications and Leadership” modules cover? Also, is the program geared more toward beginners, or is there flexibility for professionals like me who already have a solid analytics background?

Yes, this program is designed for all we start from foundations so that even if you are not having that round in that course, you will be able to understand it from the foundations only and then we will go deep into that when you are going through the course.

Got it. Could you break down the specific topics covered in the “Machine Learning for Business — Foundations” module – for example, which algorithms, tools, or case studies are included? And similarly, what does the “Advanced Analytics, AI Applications and Leadership” module focus on – are there leadership frameworks, particular industry use‑cases, or hands‑on projects? Also, do employers generally view this certification as a differentiator for someone already working in analytics?

overall.outcomeDetail: Priya did not agree to pay the 4000‑rupee seat fee because her questions were left unanswered and she felt unheard.

rubric.label: Rapport & Opening
rubric.justification: Counsellor responded with a dismissive remark and no greeting or empathy.

rubric.label: Needs Discovery
rubric.justification: No questions were asked to uncover Priya's needs or background.

rubric.label: Objection Handling
rubric.justification: Counsellor did not address any of the student's concerns about structure or placement support.

rubric.label: Product Knowledge & Accuracy
rubric.justification: No product details or accurate information were provided.

rubric.label: Closing & Next Steps
rubric.justification: The call ended without any next‑step or commitment request.

rubric.label: Communication & Empathy
rubric.justification: Language was confrontational and lacked clarity or empathy.

phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Use a warm tone, acknowledge the student's situation, and build rapport.

phaseBreakdown.summary: Discovery – no probing questions were asked.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Ask open‑ended questions to understand Priya’s career goals, gaps, and expectations.

phaseBreakdown.name: Concerns and Objections
phaseBreakdown.summary: Objection handling – student asked about programme structure and placement support; counsellor gave an unrelated, hostile reply.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Provide clear, relevant answers and reassure the student about support services.

phaseBreakdown.name: Closing
phaseBreakdown.summary: Closing – call ended without next steps or commitment.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Summarize benefits, propose a follow‑up, and ask for a small commitment (e.g., seat fee).

strengths.quote: Yes, Priya, welcome to the counseling.

improvements.quote: I don't know what do you want from me? I want to lower my score.
improvements.suggestion: Prepare concise talking points about programme structure, beginner‑friendly design, and placement assistance.

improvements.quote: I don't know what do you want from me? I want to lower my score.

transcript.text: Hi, I’m Priya, currently taking a break after a few months of focusing on family responsibilities. I cleared the qualifier test because I’m looking for a structured way to restart my career.
Yes, Priya, welcome to the counseling. I will be helping out with all the stuff you will be needing. And let's begin.

Got it. Could you tell me how the programme is structured for someone like me who’s been out of a routine for a while? Is it beginner‑friendly and does it include any placement support or guidance for restarting a career?
I don't know what do you want from me? I want to lower my score.

I’m just trying to understand how the program works for someone re‑entering the workforce. Could you explain what kind of support is provided for building a routine, and whether there’s any placement assistance or employer networking included?

overall.outcomeDetail: The student did not agree to pay the ₹4,000 seat‑block nor commit to any next step.

rubric.label: Closing & Payment Ask
rubric.justification: No payment or next‑step ask was made; the call ended without a closing attempt.

transcript.text: Hi, I’m Rahul, I’ve been working in operations for the past 5 years, handling logistics and inventory. I took the qualifier test because I’m curious if I can shift into a data/analytics role.
Hi Rahul! Great to hear you cleared the qualifier. Operations to data is a very common switch — could you tell me what tools you already use day to day, like Excel or any reporting dashboards?

I mainly use Excel for daily tracking and some basic PowerBI dashboards we get from the analytics team, but I haven’t built any reports myself. Other than that, most of my work is manual entry and inventory checks.

overall.outcomeDetail: The student did not agree to pay the ₹4,000 seat‑block because no payment ask or commitment was made during the call.

rubric.label: Rapport & Opening
rubric.justification: Polite greeting ('Hi, Ananya') but no personal connection or wheel‑handing, falling short of the level‑3 anchor.

rubric.label: Needs Discovery
rubric.justification: Asked a generic question about why the call was joined but did not probe deeper into goals, current job hunt or family context—below the level‑3 standard checklist.

rubric.label: Programme Presentation
rubric.justification: No curriculum, fee, duration or format was presented; the counsellor jumped to vague placement claims, matching the level‑1 anchor.

rubric.label: Objection Handling
rubric.justification: No objections were raised and the counsellor made no pre‑emptive handling, fitting the level‑1 anchor of steam‑rolling or ignoring concerns.

rubric.label: Product Knowledge & Accuracy
rubric.justification: Provided only vague, unverified placement statements and did not share any factual course details; not false but also not the accurate, complete info of the level‑3 anchor.

rubric.label: Closing & Payment Ask
rubric.justification: No payment or next‑step ask was made at any point, aligning with the level‑1 anchor of missing a closing ask.

rubric.label: Communication & Empathy
rubric.justification: Language was polite and clear but emotionally flat, matching the level‑3 anchor.

rubric.label: Voice Delivery
rubric.justification: Delivery was steady and intelligible (197 wpm then 137 wpm) without major disfluencies, consistent with the level‑3 anchor.

drills.title: Closing with a Clear Payment Ask
drills.instruction: Practice transitioning from curriculum overview to a concise payment request: state the ₹4,000 seat‑block, explain GST‑inclusive amount, and lock a specific follow‑up time, while confirming the student’s comfort with the fee.

drills.title: Structured Programme Presentation

drills.title: Deep Discovery Probing

transcript.text: Hi, I’m Ananya Sharma, I graduated in Computer Science from NIT a couple of months ago. I took the qualifier test because I’m looking for something that could boost my chances of getting a good placement.
Hi, Ananya.
Hello! I can hear you clearly. Looking forward to hearing more.
Can you describe clearly what do you really want and why you have joined this call?

I’ve just finished my B.Tech in Computer Science and have been applying for entry‑level roles. I joined the call hoping this programme could give me an edge—maybe practical projects or a recognized certification that improves my placement prospects.

You are at the correct place Ananya, as you have said you are graduated in computer science from NIT and it's been a month only and you are already looking for some courses and we are definitely providing you good courses especially in cyber security field because as you know this field is growing very much.
I’m exploring options that can give me hands‑on experience and a credential that employers recognize. Could you tell me what typical roles or positions alumni of this cybersecurity program end up in?

it's a very good question Ananya so basically our aluminized are placed in very recognized fields for example in Google Microsoft and all are securing our future as a cybersecurity roles and they are having good packages as well

That sounds promising. Could you share how the placement support works—do you have dedicated recruiters or partnerships? Also, what does the campus immersion at IIM Ranchi involve for students in this program?

overall.outcomeDetail: The student did not agree to pay the ₹4,000 seat‑block and simply indicated limited time without committing.

rubric.label: Objection Handling
rubric.justification: No objection handling shown; the counsellor neither anticipates nor responds to any concerns.

rubric.label: Product Knowledge & Accuracy
rubric.justification: No factual course information was given, but also no false statements were made.

rubric.label: Closing & Payment Ask
rubric.justification: No payment or next‑step ask was made; the call ended without any closing action.

rubric.label: Communication & Empathy
rubric.justification: Polite and clear throughout, though emotionally flat and only offered a generic schedule adjustment.

rubric.justification: Clear and steady speech with appropriate pace; tone remained neutral without engaging inflection.

drills.title: Rapid Discovery for Time‑Pressed Professionals

transcript.text: Hi, I’m Arjun Mehta, a data analyst with about five years of experience working on reporting and dashboard automation. I took the qualifier test because I’m curious if adding cybersecurity and AI to my skill set could give me a next‑level edge.
Hi Arjun, how are you?
I’m good, thanks. I can hear you clearly, but I only have a few minutes before my next meeting.
Are you busy as of now because we can meet after an hour also if you are free after that.
I’m a bit pressed for time right now, so let’s keep it brief.

transcript.text: Hi, I’m Ramesh, final‑year CSE student. I saw the AI/ML qualifier ad and took the test just to see what it’s about.

hi Ramesh welcome to this course this is Amman and today I'll walk you through the details of the program which you might be interested in but to start off can we start off with a quick introduction of yours
Sure, I can hear you fine. I’m Ramesh, doing my final year in Computer Science, and I took the qualifier after seeing it on Instagram.
Okay, great. What about this course excited you the most?

I’m mainly curious because AI’s being talked about everywhere, and I thought it might add some useful skills to my resume before I graduate. I don’t have any solid plan yet, just want to see what it involves.
Okay, let me know if you have any question, any doubts about this course.
Can you explain how the 8–10 hours per week are split—are they live sessions, recorded modules, or a mix?
